records.ety: [Cf. Gr. <?/ bent so as to be convex in front.]

defs: A hump-backed person; -- so called sportively.

records.ety: [OE. lord, laverd, loverd, AS. hlāford, for hlāfweard, i. e., bread keeper; hlāf bread, loaf + weardian to look after, to take care of, to ward. See Loaf, and Ward to guard, and cf. Laird, Lady.]

defs: One who has power and authority; a master; a ruler; a governor; a prince; a proprietor, as of a manor.
quotes: But now I was the lord Of this fair mansion.
Man over men He made not lord.

defs: A titled nobleman., whether a peer of the realm or not; a bishop, as a member of the House of Lords; by courtesy; the son of a duke or marquis, or the eldest son of an earl; in a restricted sense, a baron, as opposed to noblemen of higher rank.
A title bestowed on the persons above named; and also, for honor, on certain official persons; as, lord advocate, lord chamberlain, lord chancellor, lord chief justice, etc.

quotes: Thou worthy lord Of that unworthy wife that greeteth thee.

defs: One of whom a fee or estate is held; the male owner of feudal land; as, the lord of the soil; the lord of the manor.

notes: ☞ When Lord, in the Old Testament, is printed in small capitals, it is usually equivalent to Jehovah, and might, with more propriety, be so rendered.

defs: To invest with the dignity, power, and privileges of a lord.
To rule or preside over as a lord.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Lorded; p. pr. & vb. n. Lording.]

defs: To play the lord; to domineer; to rule with arbitrary or despotic sway; -- sometimes with over; and sometimes with it in the manner of a transitive verb; as, rich students lording it over their classmates.
quotes: The whiles she lordeth in licentious bliss.
I see them lording it in London streets.
And lorded over them whom now they serve.
